content:
12 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Here’s a quick experiment. Close your eyes for five seconds and think about what you’re having for dinner.
Did one single thought march through your head in a straight line? Or did a few things collide at once — what’s in the fridge, what you ate yesterday, whether you’re too lazy to cook — and then your brain mashed all of that together into a decision?
That messy, branching, reconnecting style of thinking is how real cognition works. For a long time, AI was only allowed to think in a straight line. Graph of Thoughts — introduced in 2023 by a research team at ETH Zurich — is the framework that finally changed that.
This isn’t just a conceptual article. By the end, you’ll understand the idea from first principles, know how to actually build it in Python, recognize the exact problems you’ll hit along the way, and — crucially — know when it’s the wrong tool entirely.
Stop and think: When was the last time you solved a hard problem by walking in a perfectly straight line from question to answer? Or did you bounce around, try partial solutions, and combine them? Hold that feeling — it’s the whole intuition behind this framework.

Part 1: The Backstory — How We Got Here
Before you can understand Graph of Thoughts, you need to understand what came before it and exactly why each step was an upgrade.
Step 1: Chain of Thought (2022)
The first big leap was Chain of Thought, published by Google researchers in 2022. The idea was simple: instead of asking an AI for a direct answer, you tell it to “show its work” step by step.
Without CoT, an AI asked “Roger has 5 tennis balls. He buys 2 more cans of 3 balls each. How many does he have?” would often just guess. With CoT, it writes:
Roger starts with 5 balls → buys 2 cans × 3 balls = 6 new balls → 5 + 6 = 11 total.
Forcing the model to reason out loud made it dramatically better at multi-step problems. But a chain has a fatal weakness baked into its name: it’s a single, unbroken line. One bad step poisons everything downstream. There’s no branching, no backtracking, no safety net.
Step 2: Tree of Thoughts (2023)
Tree of Thoughts fixed the backtracking problem by letting the AI explore multiple paths simultaneously, like a chess player imagining several possible moves before committing. At each step, instead of picking one next thought, the AI generates several candidates, evaluates which ones look most promising, prunes the weak ones, and keeps growing the strong branches.
This was genuinely powerful. Dead ends stop being catastrophic — you just snip that branch and try another. But trees have one rule that turns out to be the bottleneck: branches can never reconnect. Once two paths diverge, they’re isolated from each other forever.
Stop and think: Imagine Friend A has figured out the left half of a puzzle. Friend B has figured out the right half. In a tree, these two people can never even see each other’s work. Doesn’t that seem like a waste of perfectly good progress?
Step 3: Graph of Thoughts (2023)
That wasted progress is exactly the gap Graph of Thoughts closes.

Part 2: The Core Idea — What a Graph Actually Lets You Do
A graph is just dots (called nodes) connected by lines (called edges). Unlike a tree, those lines can go anywhere — sideways, backward, across disconnected branches. Nothing is permanently isolated.
In Graph of Thoughts, every node is a unit of the AI’s reasoning — one “thought.” Edges represent dependencies between thoughts. And crucially, the graph can do something trees fundamentally cannot: two separate nodes can merge into a single new, better node.
This operation is called aggregation, and it’s the heart of the whole framework.
Here’s the toolbox of four moves the AI can make:
Generate — create a new thought from an existing one. Like brainstorming a fresh idea from an old one.
Aggregate — merge two or more thoughts into one combined, improved thought. Like two teammates who worked on separate halves of a project combining their work into a final deliverable.
Refine — loop back to an existing thought and improve it based on new information. Like rewriting a paragraph after a friend points out it doesn’t make sense. This is something a tree can never do — once a branch exists, it’s permanent.
Score — evaluate how good a thought is, and prune low-quality ones before they waste further computation.
Stop and think: When you rewrite an essay or redo a drawing, do you throw it out and start a brand-new branch from scratch? Or do you loop back and improve the same piece? That looping-back instinct is exactly what “refine” enables in a graph.

Part 3: A Worked Example — Sorting 64 Numbers
Let’s make this completely concrete. The original ETH Zurich researchers used sorting as a benchmark, so let’s walk through it exactly.
Problem: sort a list of 64 random numbers. If you ask an AI to do this in one pass with Chain of Thought, it often drops numbers, duplicates them, or loses track of its position. Context windows have limits. Working memory runs out.
A graph-based approach handles this elegantly:
Step 1 — Split. Divide the 64 numbers into four groups of 16. Each group becomes its own node, worked on independently. This is just four separate Generate operations, each solving a tractable sub-problem.
Step 2 — Sort each chunk. Each node gets sorted on its own. Sorting 16 numbers is easy and reliable. Now you have four small, clean, sorted lists as four nodes sitting side by side in the graph.
Step 3 — Aggregate (the magic move). Two sorted nodes get merged into a single new node containing a sorted list of 32. Meanwhile, the other two chunks get merged separately. This step is impossible in a tree — it requires two independent branches to fuse, which trees structurally cannot do.
Step 4 — Aggregate again. Two lists of 32 merge into one final sorted list of 64.
Step 5 — Score and refine. A validation step checks: did any numbers get dropped or duplicated in the merges? If something’s off, just the problematic node gets refined — not the entire graph from scratch.
The results from the original paper were striking. Graph of Thoughts produced higher-quality sorted outputs than Tree of Thoughts while using less computation overall, because it didn’t duplicate work across branches that were secretly solving overlapping portions of the same problem.

Part 4: How It’s Wired Together — The Four Components
A graph of floating thoughts is useless without something directing traffic. The framework splits responsibility into four roles:
The Prompter handles what actually gets sent to the language model at each step. It knows how to phrase a Generate request, an Aggregate request, and a Refinement request — all differently, all precisely. Writing good Prompter templates is where most of the craft lives in a real implementation.
The Parser reads the raw model output and extracts the actual structured thought from it. Language models are verbose — they add preamble, caveats, and formatting. The Parser’s job is to strip all that away and give you just the clean data the graph needs to work with.
The Scoring Module evaluates whether a given thought is actually any good. For sorting, this is objective — count the number of misplaced elements. For more subjective tasks, this module is where the design gets hard. It could call the LLM again to self-evaluate, or apply a deterministic function, or use a secondary model.
The Controller is the team lead. It sees the whole graph, decides what operation to run next, picks which nodes to operate on, and decides when to stop. This is where the “intelligence” of the system lives beyond the LLM itself — the Controller encodes your strategy for solving the problem.
Think of it as a kitchen: the Prompter is the recipe card, the Parser tastes the dish and describes it, the Scoring Module is the critic deciding if it’s good enough, and the Controller is the head chef deciding what goes on the stove next.

Part 5: Let’s Actually Build It
Enough theory. Here’s how you implement it.
Installation
The official implementation from ETH Zurich lives at github.com/spcl/graph-of-thoughts and is available on PyPI:
pip install graph-of-thoughtsYou’ll also need an API key for your language model of choice in a config.json file in your working directory.
The Minimal Working Example
from examples.sorting.sorting_032 import SortingPrompter, SortingParser, utils
from graph_of_thoughts import controller, language_models, operations# Your input problem
to_be_sorted = "[0, 2, 6, 3, 8, 7, 1, 1, 6, 7, 9, 3, 0, 1, 7, 9, 3, 5, 1, 3]"# Step 1: Define the Graph of Operations (your strategy)
gop = operations.GraphOfOperations()
gop.append_operation(operations.Generate(1, 1))
gop.append_operation(operations.Score(1, False, utils.num_errors))
gop.append_operation(operations.GroundTruth(utils.test_sorting))# Step 2: Connect to your language model
lm = language_models.ChatGPT("config.json", model_name="chatgpt")# Step 3: Create the Controller with all four components wired together
ctrl = controller.Controller(
lm,
gop,
SortingPrompter(),
SortingParser(),
{
"original": to_be_sorted,
"current": "",
"method": "got"
}
)# Step 4: Run it
ctrl.run()
ctrl.output_graph("output_got.json")Building a Custom Prompter
The Prompter is where you do most of the real work when adapting GoT to your own problem. Here’s the shape of a custom one:
from graph_of_thoughts import prompterclass MyPrompter(prompter.Prompter): def generate_prompt(self, num_branches, original, current, method, **kwargs):
return f"""
You are solving: {original}
Current progress: {current}
Generate {num_branches} distinct next steps.
Return each as a JSON object on its own line.
""" def aggregation_prompt(self, state_dicts, **kwargs):
thoughts = [s["current"] for s in state_dicts]
return f"""
You have {len(thoughts)} partial solutions:
{chr(10).join(thoughts)}
Combine them into one single, best solution.
Return only the merged result as JSON.
""" def improve_prompt(self, **kwargs):
return f"""
Here is a solution: {kwargs.get('current')}
It has the following problems: {kwargs.get('errors')}
Fix only those problems. Return the corrected solution as JSON.
"""Building a Custom Parser
from graph_of_thoughts import parser
import jsonclass MyParser(parser.Parser): def parse_generate_answer(self, state, texts):
new_states = []
for text in texts:
try:
clean = text.strip().replace("```json", "").replace("```", "")
parsed = json.loads(clean)
new_state = state.copy()
new_state["current"] = parsed
new_states.append(new_state)
except json.JSONDecodeError:
print(f"Warning: could not parse response: {text[:100]}")
continue
return new_states def parse_aggregation_answer(self, original_states, text):
try:
clean = text.strip().replace("```json", "").replace("```", "")
merged = json.loads(clean)
new_state = original_states[0].copy()
new_state["current"] = merged
return [new_state]
except json.JSONDecodeError:
return [original_states[0]] # Fall back to best original def parse_improve_answer(self, state, text):
try:
clean = text.strip().replace("```json", "").replace("```", "")
improved = json.loads(clean)
new_state = state.copy()
new_state["current"] = improved
return new_state
except json.JSONDecodeError:
return state # Return unchanged if parse failsDesigning a Real Graph of Operations
The quickstart example uses a single Generate. Here’s a more realistic GoT strategy — for example, summarizing a document across multiple sections:
gop = operations.GraphOfOperations()# Generate 3 independent thoughts in parallel
gop.append_operation(operations.Generate(1, 3))# Score all three
gop.append_operation(operations.Score(1, False, my_scoring_fn))# Keep only the top 2
gop.append_operation(operations.KeepBestN(2))# Aggregate the two best into one combined output
gop.append_operation(operations.Aggregate(2))# Refine the merged result once
gop.append_operation(operations.Improve())# Final score to validate
gop.append_operation(operations
